blocks.html:
Extraction means turning messy documents — invoices, contracts, listings — into clean structured data. The goal is not "valid JSON." It's data that is syntactically valid, semantically correct, traceable to its source, and safe for downstream systems. A schema can prove a field is a number; it can never prove the document actually said that number.

The most important design insight is about incentives: a required field the source might not contain pressures the model to invent a value. If listings often omit lot size but your schema demands one, you will get plausible fabricated lot sizes — schema-legal and wrong. The fix is making absence representable: nullable fields, empty-vs-null distinctions ("no pros mentioned" vs "pros not addressed"), an unclear enum value for ambiguous cases, and an other + detail-field escape hatch for evolving categories.

Tempted to add a second "verification" LLM call? It's usually inferior: it adds cost, can rationalize the original invention, and leaves the schema pressure in place. Fix the incentive; keep verification passes for sampling-based audits.

A contract says the notice period is 60 days — then Amendment 2 changes it to 45. A single termination_notice_days scalar is the wrong schema. High-stakes extraction carries provenance: value, source_location ("Amendment 2, section 4"), source_quote, and effective date, with original and amended values captured separately. Reviewers can then audit every choice against the actual text. (Note: API citations and strict JSON structured outputs may be incompatible — represent source locations as schema fields instead.)

Validation and scale patterns the exam probes:

  • Semantic validation beyond the schema: totals reconcile, dates in range, IDs match known formats. Add reconciliation fields (calculated_total, stated_total, totals_match) so mismatches flag mechanically.
  • Correction requests: source + previous extraction + exact errors. The one unfixable case: the information was never in the provided context — no retry helps; retrieve the source or route to human review.
  • Scattered long documents: when the doc fits in context but facts are buried, a pre-extraction pass (surface decisions, amounts, dates into an intermediate) beats both chunking (loses cross-section links) and more few-shot examples (don't help find needles).
  • Automation thresholds: segment accuracy by document type, field, and confidence band first — 97% overall can hide an 80% segment. Keep stratified sampling after launch.
  • Batch: join results by custom_id, resubmit only failures, route urgent documents to real-time per-document.

items: Required-but-absent fields manufacture hallucinations; make absence representable.
Schema compliance ≠ source truth — semantic validation in code is still mandatory.
Amendments and conflicts demand provenance fields, not scalars.
Scattered facts in long docs → pre-extraction pass into a structured intermediate.
Segment accuracy before auto-approving anything; aggregates lie.
